data.title: Hess Midstream: The Issue Continues To Be The Bakken Upstream Business
data.description:
The last article (and articles before that) has long mentioned that Hess never achieved a satisfactory level of profitability in the Bakken. That is way more important than anything Hess Midstream (HESM) may have going for it. If your major customer is not seeing adequate levels of profitability, it places the whole business model (in this case, servicing that acreage) at risk. Hess originally made a whole lot of money taking Hess Midstream public and then periodically selling stock. That is a further warning that operations were going to become an issue sooner or later.

The last conference call noted that the capital budget was down as well. As a result, there have been a whole lot of opinions about how Hess Midstream's free cash flow is growing from the lower capital budget combined with how that makes the distribution picture even better.

But no one is focusing on the fact that the Bakken acreage was fairly high-cost acreage. When I covered Hess and Hess Midstream, I focused on the fact that there was a lack of profitability in the Bakken. In fact, before the Guyana discovery, Hess itself, back in 2015, was not all that profitable. What made Hess an investment consideration was the ability to cash the checks generated by the operations (with Exxon Mobil (XOM) as the operator) of Guyana as production grew. When Hess was acquired by Chevron (CVX), this was actually the source of the Hess profits.

Chevron Opinion

Shown below is the latest statement by Hess Midstream about the Bakken:

Note that during the conference call, Chevron management still intends to hold the volumes produced at 200,000 BOED. Also note that the number of rigs has now declined to 3. This appears to be similar to what Chevron management is stating.

Here is the latest statement about the situation by Chevron:

This is really sugarcoating a difficult situation. But there is confirmation from both sides that there will be no volume growth for the time being. Instead, driving cash flow and profitability is the issue.

What makes the situation tough is that the Bakken is generally not seen as the low-cost basin. That title has gone to the Permian Basin for some time. That makes the overall upstream business serviced by Hess Midstream a questionable proposition for the time being.

Furthermore, the lack of production growth caps future prospects at some point. The quarterly dividend increase is a current "diversion" designed to take the concentration away from the no-growth issue. Right now, it seems to be working.

The even larger consideration is that 200,000 BOED is peanuts to Chevron. But it is the whole business model for Hess Midstream. As an investor, the major question revolves around the future of the Bakken business because of the subpar profitability reported before the acquisition.

At the very least, Chevron will allocate capital to the most profitable areas of the company. There is a very real possibility that this part of the company may eventually not compete for capital.

Stock Price

To a certain extent, the Hess Midstream stock price reflects the uncertainty revolving around the business model.

Despite all the good news, the stock price has clearly stalled. It is, in fact, very easy to see the difference in the stock price action once Chevron acquired Hess and announced that one rig would be taken from the Bakken. Now it looks like another rig is leaving the Bakken, coming down to 3 active rigs. This part of the cost savings will affect depreciation. But a whole lot more cost reduction is needed besides this.

What is needed is for Bakken wells to make some profitability progress, exceeding the more profitable Permian business so that the acreage can successfully compete for capital in the future (and even grow production). Right now, there is no indication in the statements above from either company that the acreage is making that kind of progress.

Chevron is evidently trying for a certain profitability level. That is to be expected after an acquisition. But there is a world of difference between adequate profitability and low cost. The low-cost operations will get the lion's share of any capital budget.

Finances

Hess Midstream's finances have always been excellent. As has been noted many times in the past, the long-term debt ratio goal is roughly 3.0, which is extremely conservative for midstream companies. Additionally, the rising free cash flow has allowed the dividend to increase at a slow rate quarterly.

If the only consideration was the business itself, this would be an extremely attractive business.

But midstream companies frequently see trouble coming from the parent company when (as in this case) roughly 90% of the business comes from one customer. In this case, the trouble comes from the lack of profitability of the upstream business on this acreage.

That consideration really nullifies (and more) the advantages of a quarterly increasing dividend combined with a great debt ratio because there might not be a future upstream business if things do not change materially. Another possibility is that the midstream fee structure could change materially to help the upstream business become more profitable. None of this is potentially good future news for the midstream investors.

Risks

There is always the risk that a technological advance makes the Bakken a low-cost basin once again. The company (now private) that seemed to have the best Bakken acreage was unconventional pioneer Continental Resources. Everyone else got what was left. What Chevron can do about the situation long-term is anyone's guess.

It is possible that Hess Midstream could change strategy to acquire some diversification and growth prospects elsewhere. Right now, that does not seem to be a consideration (as was implied in the latest quarterly report).

Summary

Hess Midstream still has to contend with the fact that Chevron is working on the profitability of the Bakken. But until it is evident that the profitability in the Bakken is satisfactory to Chevron so that there is a decent future, this remains a very strong sell as an income idea.

The risk to midstream companies nearly always comes from the controlling parent company in a situation like this. There is a very big threat to the midstream business when the parent company is not making money on the acreage.

If anyone reviews my older articles on Hess that are under my profile and now combined with the Chevron articles, one can easily see that Hess itself was not all that profitable until the Guyana discovery. Even after that discovery, the rest of Hess remained near the breakeven point (mostly).

Until Chevron demonstrates and states that the Bakken acreage is profitable enough to remain part of the Chevron portfolio of projects, this is an extremely dangerous long-term idea for buy-and-hold income investors. But that would mean a big change from the past (including the Hess past as well).

Now, with commodity prices elevated, it would be a good time to consider exiting this company and heading to something much safer.

data.title: Praxis: Strong Buy As Relutrigine Submission Accepted Plus Expansion Potential
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Praxis Precision Medicines, Inc. (PRAX), it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Praxis: Moving To Strong Buy Based On Ulixacaltamide Success In Treating ET." With respect to this article, I mentioned that the company had achieved positive results from both of its phase 3 studies of the ESSENTIAL3 program using its drug ulixacaltamide for the treatment of patients with essential tremor [ET]. This was a huge event because it put into motion the next expected catalyst that was set for Q4 of 2025, which was that the company was to hold a pre-NDA meeting with the FDA to discuss the possibility of being able to file for regulatory approval of this drug.

I'm happy to report that this meeting with the FDA went well in December of 2025 and that the company was able to submit its NDA of ulixacaltamide for the treatment of these ET patients. The last time I rated this stock, I gave it a Strong Buy rating, and I'm going to stick to this rating again. Not just because the company was able to submit its NDA of ulixacaltamide for ET.

It goes deeper than that, and it is because the company also had another huge positive update relating to its pipeline after I had written about it. It released positive interim results from the phase 2/3 EMBOLD registrational cohort using its drug relutrigine for the treatment of SCN2A and SCN8A developmental and epileptic encephalopathy [DEE]. The interim study was stopped early due to overwhelming results from this EMBOLD trial.

The thing is that not only was Praxis able to submit a New Drug Application [NDA] of relutrigine for the treatment of these specific SCN2A and SCN8A patients, but it also had it accepted by the FDA with priority review with a Prescription Drug User Fee Act [PDUFA] target action date of September 27, 2026. The truth is that this program of targeting DEEs has two expansion opportunities, which are other reasons for my Strong Buy rating. The first expansion of which is that because relutrigine targets the hyperactivity of sodium channels observed in genetically driven DEEs, it is able to target a broad patient population.

This is being evaluated in the EMERALD study, where relutrigine is being explored for broad DEEs beyond that of SCN2A and SCN8A. The second expansion is that another drug candidate in its pipeline, known as elsunersen [antisense oligonucleotide], is being developed for the targeting of early-onset SCN2A DEE with gain-of-function [GoF] mutations in the ongoing EMBRAVE3 registrational study. Top-line results from this particular study are expected in 2027, and if positive, they would allow for registration of this drug candidate for the treatment of these GoF mutation SCN2A DEE patients. With two NDAs submitted from its pipeline, plus the potential to expand to the targeting of broad DEE patients using relutrigine, I believe that this stock should remain as a Strong Buy rating.

DEE Program Has A Few Expansion Opportunities In Place To Increase Target Market

As I noted above, Praxis Precision Medicines has already been able to submit its NDA of ulixacaltamide for the treatment of patients with essential tremor [ET] to the FDA. As that program came along, the company was also able to submit another NDA as part of its pipeline, which was its drug relutrigine for the treatment of patients with SCN2A and SCN8A DEEs. This was based on strong data that was released from the EMBOLD study. Before going into detail about the data released from this study, plus any catalysts to come about from it, I believe that it is first important to go over what DEEs are and what the possible market opportunity could end up being.

Developmental and epileptic encephalopathy [DEE] is characterized as a group of epilepsies where both seizures and developmental delay occur. The "developmental delay" component of this is referred to as epileptic encephalopathy. The thing is that it typically begins in early childhood, and the main issue with this is that it causes seizures that are both frequent and severe. One other issue, which relutrigine might be able to help with, is the fact that these seizures are also drug-resistant.

It is important to note that some DEEs come about because of mutations in the sodium channel genes of a person. In the case I'm talking about here, which the company is going after, there are mutations of the SCN2A and SCN8A genes. These mutations are bad because these genes are responsible for developing proteins that control electric signals in the brain. Without them, a person enters into a state of hyperactive sodium channels, which leads to DEEs. Some symptoms that these patients experience are:

  • Autism
  • Movement disorder
  • Cognitive disability
  • Drug resistance.

As far as the target market goes, it is expected that there are about 5,000 SCN2A/SCN8A DEE patients in the United States. This translates to a potential market opportunity of about $500 million for this territory. This is a good starting point, but the beauty of relutrigine is that it can go after a broad set of DEEs. Why is that? The reason why is because a majority of seizure etiologies converge at sodium channels [Praxis Precision Medicines Corporate Presentation February 2026, slide #14]. This is crucial because this means that the company is potentially able to help a broad set of these DEE patients. More about this below, but the company is actually evaluating the use of relutrigine in the ongoing EMERALD study. The reason why I mentioned this is because if the company can successfully gain FDA approval of this drug for SCN2A and SCN8A DEEs, plus expand to broad DEEs with an sNDA thereafter, then it expects that it could target a >$5 billion market opportunity [Praxis Precision Medicines Corporate Presentation February 2026, slide #5].

Going back to this program of relutrigine targeting SCN2A and SCN8A DEE patients, this was made possible thanks to positive results from the phase 2/3 EMBOLD study. The beauty of this program is that highly positive interim results warranted the Data Monitoring Committee [DMC] to stop the trial early due to efficacy. At that time, Praxis also mentioned that it was to meet with the FDA to confirm a path forward for the use of relutrigine to treat these specific DEE patients. The double-blind, placebo-controlled, randomized study enrolled a total of 16 patients with early-onset SCN2A and SCN8A DEE. These participants were randomized 1:1 to receive either of the following dosing schedules:

  • Relutrigine once daily [QD] for 16 weeks
  • Relutrigine QD for 12 weeks and then matching placebo for 4 weeks.

The main result to pay attention to is the randomized, double-blind portion of the study where relutrigine-treated patients were able to achieve a placebo-adjusted 46% reduction in motor seizure frequency. Not only that, but what's impressive is that about 33% of these patients ended up being seizure-free after the 16-week period as well. There were no drug-related serious adverse events [SAEs] to mention, and the adverse events [AEs] that happened were mild to moderate.

These positive results from the interim analysis of the EMBOLD study, again stopped early due to efficacy, are what allowed the company to submit an NDA of relutrigine for the treatment of these SCN2A/SNC8A DEE patients to the FDA. This U.S. agency not only accepted the NDA of this drug for the treatment of these specific DEE patients but also set a priority review PDUFA target action date of September 27, 2026. What makes this good for the patients is that, should this drug be approved, it would be the first one for them. That's because there are no currently approved therapies to treat these SCN2A and SCN8A DEE patients. It would be the first disease-modifying therapy for them.

The bullish nature of this company, as I highlighted in this article, is the nature of it being able to target the hyperexcitability state of sodium channels, giving it broad potential. This means that it can go beyond that of only targeting certain gene mutations that cause the DEE. With that being said, it is in the process of exploring the use of relutrigine for broad DEEs in the ongoing EMERALD study. If this study ends up being successful, plus barring that relutrigine is approved for U.S. marketing of targeting patients with SCN2A and SCN8A DEE, then it could file a supplemental new drug application [sNDA] for this drug to expand its label. Full enrollment of this EMERALD study is expected to happen in the 2nd half of 2026, with data to be released from it thereafter. Thus, the sNDA might potentially be submitted in 2027.

The second expansion opportunity is actually using an entirely different drug from its pipeline, which is antisense oligonucleotide [ASO] elsunersen, being developed for the treatment of patients with early seizure onset SCN2A DEE. There are two things to mention about this program. The first of which is that the company just released a set of results from Part A of the phase 1/2 randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled EMBRAVE study. It stated that elsunersen was able to achieve a 77% placebo-adjusted seizure reduction from baseline. This is pretty good data, and why this program is coming along well, just like relutrigine.

The thing is that the company had another positive development for this program that came its way. After meeting with the FDA in December of 2025, it was noted that the company could remove the sham control arm as part of the other ongoing EMBRAVE3 study for this program. Barring that this study remains on track, the company believes that it is going to be able to release data from it in 2027.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, Praxis Precision Medicines had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $926.1 million as of December 31, 2025. The truth is that this company has plenty of cash on hand, especially since it was able to raise additional cash in January of 2026. This is where it enacted a public offering to raise an estimated $575 million. It even granted the underwriters a 30-day option to purchase up to an additional 331,800 shares as well. The total gross proceeds raised from this offering were $621.2 million.

With the cash of $926.1 million as of December 31, 2025, plus the amount raised through the January 2026 offering, it believes that it will be able to fund its operations into 2028.

The company burns approximately $97 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $77.5 million in R&D expenses and then $19.5 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Praxis Precision Medicines. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the submitted NDA of ulixacaltamide for the treatment of patients with ET. For the time being, this regulatory application was submitted to the FDA after a positive pre-NDA meeting with it in December of 2025. The risk here is that there is no assurance that this regulatory application is going to be accepted by the FDA in its current form, nor that the company will receive U.S. marketing approval of ulixacaltamide for the treatment of ET patients.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the development of relutrigine for the treatment of patients with SCN2A and SNC8A DE. The company was able to stop its trial early due to efficacy and submit its NDA of this drug to the FDA. A priority review PDUFA target action date of September 27, 2026, was set by the agency to review the drug by.

The risk here is that there is no assurance that the FDA is going to approve relutrigine for the treatment of these patients with SCN2A/SCN8A DEE for starters. Another risk to consider here would be the first expansion opportunity I mentioned above, which is the development of this drug to target broad DEE [pan-DEE] patients. This is happening with the ongoing EMERALD study that is in place. The risk here is that the company mentions that should this study have a successful outcome, then it would serve as the basis for it to be able to file an sNDA of relutrigine to target a broad group of DEE patients. The risk here is that there is no guarantee that the final results are going to be positive, despite the fact that the company mentioned that its drug is capable of targeting multiple etiologies of this disease.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the development of ASO elsunersen for the treatment of early seizure onset SCN2A gain of function [GoF] DEE patients being advanced in two studies. The company is currently exploring the use of this drug candidate in the EMBRAVE3 registrational trial treating these specific DEE patients. It already was able to show that it was capable of achieving a 77% placebo-adjusted seizure reduction from baseline from Part A of the Phase 1/2 EMBRAVE study.

The risk here is that there is no assurance that the company will achieve a positive final outcome from the EMBRAVE3 registrational study, nor that the efficacy of what was achieved beforehand is going to be closely similar.

Conclusion

The final conclusion is that Praxis Precision Medicines continues to deliver promising results from its pipeline with a variety of drugs. With respect to ulixacaltamide for the treatment of patients with ET, it was able to submit an NDA to the FDA. In terms of the program of advancing the use of relutrigine for the treatment of patients with SCN2A and SCN8A DEEs, it not only had its NDA accepted by the FDA but was granted a priority review target action PDUFA date of September 27, 2026.

Hopefully, this goes well, because it is already in the process of evaluating the use of this drug for a broad set of DEE patients in DEE in the EMERALD study. What's the point of this? As I mentioned above, being able to go after this broad set of DEE patients will allow it to expand its market opportunity to be >$5 billion. Another item to mention is that relutrigine for the targeting of SCN2A/SCN8A DEE has been granted Breakthrough Therapy Designation [BTD], Rare Pediatric Disease Designation, and Orphan Drug Designation [ODD].

In essence, it has the chance to not only be the first approved therapy for these specific DEE patients, but it could also allow Praxis Precision Medicines to obtain a Pediatric Review Voucher [PRV]. It can either choose to keep this to speed up the review of another drug or end up selling it to generate cash. With everything I mentioned above, I believe that this stock should remain as a Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Synchrony Financial: Sell-Off Presents Great Entry Point For Shares (Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Synchrony Financial (SYF) is a consumer lending depositor bank. The bank uses depositor money to lend out using credit cards and consumer-based financing. I’ve been a fan of the bank’s preferred shares, last writing about them in June. Now, with the current rate and macro environment, and after a sell-off to start the year, I believe the bank’s shares are worth buying.

Synchrony Financial Earnings

Synchrony Financial is different from a traditional depositor bank. As opposed to mortgages and business loans, Synchrony specializes in high-yield consumer loans and private-label credit cards, which gives it a much higher asset yield than most banks. The bank’s asset yield in the fourth quarter rose by 10 basis points, finishing at 19.07%. Due to the nature of its business, Synchrony’s asset yields are less influenced by rate policy, as evidenced by the fourth quarter asset yield being the highest in the post-pandemic era.

On the other hand, the bank’s borrowing yield has managed to decline thanks to the recent monetary policy moves by the Federal Reserve. Borrowing yields fell by 13 basis points in the fourth quarter to 4.07%. The changes in asset and borrowing yields have allowed the net interest spread to reach 15% for the first time since 2022. Net interest margin, which incorporates leverage into the calculation, rose to 15.83%, which was the highest level since 2019.

The trends in yields also translated somewhat over to interest income and expense. Interest income has had inconsistent growth patterns over the last couple of years and was essentially flat in the fourth quarter at $5.7 billion, which was slightly higher than the same quarter a year ago.

Interest expense declined to $973 million in the fourth quarter, which was the lowest quarterly level since the second quarter of 2023. Interest expense has declined by $145 million compared to the fourth quarter of 2024. While the bank has struggled to maintain consistent interest income, the decline in interest expense has allowed for net interest income (interest income less interest expense) to grow for three consecutive quarters to $4.76 billion, the highest level in at least six years.

Changes In Loans And Deposits

Part of the difficulty in growing interest income for Synchrony Financial has come in the bank’s inability to consistently grow lending. The bank did achieve 4% growth in lending in the fourth quarter, which was impressive. This growth was offset by a 5.3% decline in the first quarter, followed by low growth in the next two quarters. For the year, Synchrony Financial’s loan growth edged down by 0.5%.

Deposit growth was also shaky for Synchrony Financial. In the fourth quarter, deposits grew by 1.6%. Despite the fourth-quarter growth, deposit declines of 1.4% and 2.9% in the preceding two quarters influenced the 1.1% decline in deposits for the year. While the lack of growth in loans and deposits may seem disappointing, it is impressive that Synchrony Financial has managed to grow net interest income without growing the core components of its balance sheet.

One risk that investors should monitor regarding Synchrony Financial is the balance of loans to deposits, measured in the loan-to-deposit ratio. Synchrony Financial’s loan-to-deposit ratio sits at 115%, which is exceptionally high compared to other banks. This means that for every dollar Synchrony has in deposits, it has lent out $1.15.

How can a bank lend out more than it carries in deposits? This is accomplished through external financing, which is a more expensive form of financing than deposits. For Synchrony Financial, external financing has remained steady between $14.5 and $17 billion over the last few years. A decline in external financing could further support profitability, with the easiest way to accomplish this coming in deposit growth outperforming loan growth.

Risks To Synchrony Financial

Due to its nature as a consumer lender, investors need to monitor loan performance for Synchrony Financial carefully. The bank has had a steady stream of 90-day or more delinquent loans, comprising slightly more than 2% of gross loans over the last few years. Fortunately, the bank keeps an aggressive allowance for credit losses to shield earnings from bad loans. Presently, that allowance stands at 10% of gross loans, providing a nearly five-to-one ratio of coverage.

While it is related to loan performance, I believe the health of the labor market represents a unique risk factor for the bank. Should the labor market weaken and the unemployment rate rise, the growth of consumer loan delinquencies will follow. This could have a material impact on Synchrony Financial’s earnings. Therefore, investors should periodically check on the condition of the labor market as a possible leading indicator for delinquency growth.

Conclusion

Synchrony Financial has managed to grow net interest income without growing its balance sheet. The consumer lending bank has also kept its delinquency rates under control in the face of higher long-term interest rates. With a cheap source of capital in deposits, I believe the bank is set up well to grow earnings for investors; therefore, the recent sell-off represents a great entry point for the stock.

data.title: Indicators Suggest The Market Likely Hasn't Hit Bottom Yet
data.description:
By Brian Levitt, Chief Global Market Strategist and Head of Strategy & Insights

When your background is in macroeconomic and market strategy, there’s an unstated expectation that you can opine on everything. For example, I wasn’t a microbiologist in 2020 during the COVID-19 pandemic, although I often felt like I was struggling to play one on television. In much the same way, I’m not a military strategist today - nor an expert on the inner thinking of US President Trump, Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu, or Iranian Ayatollah Mojtaba Khomeini. It’s probably for the best. When you convince yourself that you understand the motivations and goals of political and military leaders, markets would likely have a way of humbling you quickly.

This past week was a reminder. At various points, markets signaled that the war was intensifying, then nearing an end, and intensifying again.1 Trying to trade in reaction to each headline is a recipe for being whipsawed. In periods like this, we come back to what we do best. We focus on interpreting what markets themselves are telling us.

Business cycle indicators have shown only gradual deterioration

Let’s start with the business cycle. As we’ve been saying, our preferred cyclical indicators are trending in the wrong direction, but they aren’t pointing to disaster. Credit spreads have modestly widened,2 inflation expectations have trended higher,3 and the US dollar has modestly strengthened.4 The rise in inflation expectations has led investors to expect further rate increases from the European Central Bank, the Bank of England, and even the Bank of Japan.5

Taken together, this gradual - but not meaningful - deterioration in cycle indicators suggests that markets still appear to believe in an exit ramp and an eventual resumption of the expansion once near-term uncertainty fades.

Global signals point toward a slowdown

Tactically, the picture has softened. Our short-lived global expansion signal has shifted toward a slowdown. Risk sentiment has been deteriorating,6 and while many leading indicators remained relatively stable,7 they may follow sentiment and confidence and deteriorate. We saw early evidence of that last week. Consumer sentiment weakened,8 and the Institute of Supply Management (ISM) services activity moderated.9 They weren’t recessionary readings, but momentum had faded.

Has the market bottomed? Probably not

That brings us to the weeks ahead and the question that many investors are asking. The major stock indexes have already corrected. The Dow Jones Industrial Average has fallen roughly 10%.10 The S&P 500 Index isn’t far behind.11 The MSCI All Country World (ACWI) ex USA Index is down more than 11%.12 Has the market already found its bottom? Based on our preferred market bottom indicators, the answer for now is likely no.

  • Sentiment has become less bullish, but it hasn’t reached the extremes typically associated with durable bottoms. The American Association of Individual Investors’ (AAII) Sentiment Survey showed bears outnumbering bulls by about 20 percentage points. At prior market lows, that gap has often been closer to 50.13
  • Volatility has increased, but it hasn’t surged to levels that have typically coincided with panic. The VIX, for example, hasn’t approached 40.14
  • Technicals also continued to deteriorate. The S&P 500 Index fell below its 200-day moving average, which may be an early warning rather than an all-clear signal. During the 2022 market bottom, it ultimately traded 16% below that level before finding a more solid footing.15

Of course, none of that’ll matter if there’s a social media post today calling off the war. But again, who am I to know?

What does this mean for investors?

I may still occasionally find myself playing a military strategist on television, but in practice, I remain grounded in what the market itself is signaling. In the short term, markets may still have work to do before a durable bottom is formed. Tactically, I believe a slowdown environment argues for maintaining stock exposure, but with greater emphasis on quality and more defensive areas of the market. Longer term, we may want to take solace in markets indicating that the broader cycle hasn’t yet ended and that this too shall pass.

What to watch this week

Date

Region

Event

Why it matters

April 6

Global

Easter Monday (selected markets closed)

Lower liquidity and reduced trading volumes can amplify volatility in open markets

April 7

US

Durable goods orders (Feb.)

Signals business investment trends and manufacturing momentum

April 8

US

Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting minutes (Mar.)

Provides insight into the Federal Reserve’s policy debate and future rate path

Eurozone

Retail sales (Feb.)

Key indicator of consumer demand and economic momentum

April 9

US

Personal income and spending/PCE inflation (Feb.)

Critical gauge of consumer strength and the Fed’s preferred inflation measure

US

Gross domestic product (GDP) (Q4, third estimate)

Confirms growth trends and revisions can affect macro outlook

April 10

US

Consumer Price Index (CPI) (Mar.)

Primary inflation indicator; major influence on interest rates and markets

Canada

Employment report (Mar.)

Assesses labor market strength and informs Bank of Canada policy expectations

Footnotes

1 Source: Bloomberg, L.P., April 2, 2026, based on the price movements of the S&P 500 Index for the week of March 30, 2026, which fell early in the week, recovered midweek, and plunged again at the end of the week.

2 Source: Bloomberg, L.P., April 2, 2026. Credit spreads are based on the option-adjusted spread of the Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index and the Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index.

3 Source: Bloomberg, L.P., April 2, 2026, based on the 5-year US Treasury breakeven rate. A breakeven inflation rate is a market-derived estimate of future inflation, calculated by comparing the yield on a standard government bond (nominal) to the yield on a Treasury Inflation-Protected Security (TIPS) of the same maturity.

4 Source: Bloomberg, L.P., April 2, 2026, based on the US Dollar Index, which measures the value of the US dollar versus a trade-weighted basket of currencies.

5 Source: Bloomberg, L.P., April 2, 2026, based on the central bank implied rates.

6 Source: Bloomberg, L.P., April 2, 2026, based on the one-month return of the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index compared to the S&P 500 Index.

7 Source: US Conference Board, based on the Conference Board US Leading Economic Indicator Index.

8 Source: University of Michigan, March 31, 2026

9 Source: Institute of Supply Management, Feb. 28, 2026

10 Source: Bloomberg, L.P., April 2, 2026, based on the 10.01% decline of the Dow Jones Industrial Average from the Feb. 10, 2026 high to the recent trough of March 27, 2026.

11 Source: Bloomberg, L.P., April 2, 2026, based on the 9.10% decline of the S&P 500 Index from the Jan. 27, 2026 high to the recent trough of March 27, 2026.

12 Source: Bloomberg, L.P., April 2, 2026, based on the 11.23% decline of MSCI ACWI ex-US from the Feb. 27, 2026 high to the recent trough of March 27, 2026.

13 Source: American Association of Individual Investors, April 1, 2026

14 Source: Chicago Board Options Exchange, April 1, 2026. The Chicago Board Options Exchange Volatility Index (VIX) is a financial benchmark designed to be an up-to-the-minute market estimate of the expected volatility of the S&P 500 Index and is calculated by using the midpoint of real-time S&P 500 Index option bid/ask quotes.

15 Source: Bloomberg, L.P., April 2, 2026, based on the returns of the S&P 500 Index. The market bottomed in 2022 after the S&P 500 Index had fallen 16.89% from its 200-day moving average.

Important information

Investments cannot be made directly in an index.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

The American Association of Individual Investors’ (AAII) Sentiment Survey offers insight into the opinions of individual investors by asking them their thoughts on where the market is heading in the next six months.

The Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of the US investment grade, fixed-rate bond market.

The Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index measures the investment grade, fixed-rate, taxable corporate bond market. It includes US dollar-denominated securities publicly issued by US and non-US industrial, utility, and financial issuers.

The CBOE Volatility Index® (VIX®) is a key measure of market expectations of near-term volatility conveyed by S&P 500 Index option prices. VIX is the ticker symbol for the Chicago Board Options Exchange (CBOE) Volatility Index, which shows the market’s expectation of 30-day volatility.

The Consumer Price Index (CPI) measures the change in consumer prices and is a commonly cited measure of inflation.

Credit spread is the difference in yield between bonds of similar maturity but with different credit quality.

A discount measures how much less one stock (or index) is trading compared with another stock (or index).

The Dow Jones Industrial Average is a price-weighted index of the 30 largest, most widely held stocks traded on the New York Stock Exchange.

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) is a committee of the Federal Reserve Board that meets regularly to set monetary policy, including the interest rates that are charged to banks.

Fixed income investments are subject to credit risk of the issuer and the effects of changing interest rates. Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa. An issuer may be unable to meet interest and/or principal payments, thereby causing its instruments to decrease in value and lowering the issuer’s credit rating.

Gross domestic product (GDP) is a broad indicator of a region’s economic activity, measuring the monetary value of all the finished goods and services produced in that region over a specified time period.

Inflation is the rate at which the general price level for goods and services is increasing.

The MSCI All Country World (ACWI) ex USA Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of large- and mid-cap stocks across developed and emerging markets, excluding the US. The index is computed using the net return, which withholds applicable taxes for nonresident investors.

Option-adjusted spread (OAS) is the yield spread that must be added to a benchmark yield curve to discount a security’s payments to match its market price, using a dynamic pricing model that accounts for embedded options.

The US Dollar Index measures the value of the US dollar relative to the majority of its most significant trading partners.

Purchasing Managers’ Indexes (PMI) are based on monthly surveys of companies worldwide and gauge business conditions within the manufacturing and services sectors.

The risks of investing in securities of foreign issuers can include fluctuations in foreign currencies, political and economic instability, and foreign taxation issues.

The S&P 500® Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of the US stock market.

A spread in finance is the difference between two related values, such as prices, rates, or yields.

In general, stock values fluctuate, sometimes widely, in response to activities specific to the company as well as general market, economic, and political conditions.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of April 2, 2026. These comments should not be construed as recommendations, but as an illustration of broader themes. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. data.title: Alphabet: Why Strong Money Likes It At 27x P/E
data.description:
Alphabet stock: super-investors love it at $313

I last covered Alphabet Inc. (GOOG, GOOGL) on Feb 23 with an article titled "Alphabet: SpaceX IPO, 100-Year Bond, And My New Understanding Of Its Moat.” As stated in the title, the article was triggered by Google’s SpaceX (SPACE) holdings and also new bond issuance, and it rated the stock as Buy. Since then, there have been a few new developments worth noting. In the rest of this article, I will focus on two of them: the latest changes in professional fund managers’ holdings and also the latest AI traffic data. These changes, when combined, suggest the stock’s growth potential is discounted, thus offering a favorable return/risk ratio and leading to a reiteration of my Buy rating.

Let me start with the changes of the so-called super-investors’ GOOG holdings. In my experiences, I benefited more from observing these investors’ transactions than following the overall market. The reasons are simply twofold in my mind. For these investors, A) their decisions are less biased by herd thinking, and B) their investment timeframe tends to be longer. With this background, the following screenshot shows the latest holdings of GOOG stock among super-investors tracked by DataRoma.

As seen, GOOG currently ranks top 2 in terms of ownership and top 3 in terms concentration (i.e., percentage allocation in their portfolios). Overall, a total of 29 super-investors has a GOOG position, the stock makes up more than 2.2% of their accounts, and their current hold price is ~$313. The current market price for GOOG is about $296 as of this writing, which is quite close to their holding price (about 5.5% lower) and makes their decision of particular interest.

Next, I will explain why these investors see a good deal at $313 and the return/risk curve is even more favorable at the current price of $296.

GOOG stock: growth outlook and return potential

Before going into any further details, let’s me start with a broader picture. The figure below shows my back-of-envelope estimates for GOOG’s return potential under various combinations of growth and valuation multiples. These estimates were made for a holding period of 5 years, but I believe the essence remains the safe for other holding periods if they are sufficiently long to filter out short-term noises.

I am especially interested in the total ROI potential (return on investment) for 3 P/E ratios. These ratios are 25.5x, 27.0x, and 28.2x. They are shown by the blue, green, and red curves in the chart, respectively. I am particularly interested in these 3 cases because they are GOOG’s current FWD P/E in the open market, the P/E ratio at the average holding price of $313 among super-investors, and also the current P/E ratio of the S&P 500 (SP500). Given GOOG’s scale and positioning among the SP500 (more on this in a minute), I see large odds for an exit P/E within the range of these 3 ratios.

Now, for its growth outlook, consensus estimates point to a compounded annual growth rate (CAGR) of 17.3% for the next 6 years as shown in the next chart. My personal view is that the consensus estimates are too aggressive. Based on my analysis of its ROCE (return on capital employed, which averages about 63% in recent years) and its long-term reinvestment rates (RR), my own growth projection is around 13% per annum on average. It is true that the RR is extremely high in recent years and will remain so in FY 2026 too due to the surges to AI-related capex projects. However, I expect the RR to revert to an average of 20% in the longer term, resulting in an organic growth rate of 13% on average (which equals the product of 63% ROCE and 20% RR).

With the above variances of exit P/E and growth rates anticipated above, the purple box in the figure illustrates the range of potential ROI. To wit, the upper-right corner of the purple box illustrates the best-case scenario in my view, featuring a combination of high growth (17.3% CAGR as consensus projects) and a high exit P/E ratio of 28.2x. In this case, the stock could deliver a total return of about 135% in 5 years. Similarly, the lower-left corner of the purple box illustrates the worst-case scenario (that is, in relative terms), featuring a combination of lower growth (13% CAGR per my estimate) and a lower P/E ratio of 25.5x. In this case, the stock could still deliver a total return of about 70% in 5 years.

GOOG stock: latest AI traffic data

In either case, I see a quite favorable return/risk curve under current condition. Given the many catalysts afoot, I indeed see good odds for double-digit annual EPS growth rates in the years to come. My recent articles have provided in-depth analyses on some of these catalyst ranging from its capital allocation flexibility to its in-house AI chips.

Today, I wanted to bring your attention to the momentum of its Gemini AI models. Since my last article, several digital analytic services (such as Similarweb, Statcounter, etc.) have provided updates to web traffic data, in particular traffic data surrounding AI models. After reviewing these updates, my overall impression is twofold. First, Google's search still dominates the global market share. The latest data showed an increase of 14 basis points month-over-month to a share of 90% in February 2026. Second, GOOG’s Gemeni model began to gain momentum and demonstrate faster growth compared to ChatGPT (see details quoted from the following report).

According to Similarweb data, all major AI platforms saw varying degrees of month-over-month growth in global average daily web visits during February. Google led significantly with 290 million daily visits, up 6% year-over-year and 4% month-over-month. ChatGPT recorded 197 million daily visits, up 42% year-over-year and 7% month-over-month. Gemini had 79 million daily visits, surging 667% year-over-year and increasing 17% month-over-month.

Other risks and final thoughts

With these developments, I see a powerful and positive interplay among GOOG’s existing business segments and new segments. In the case of search and AI, GOOG enjoys a virtuous cycle where each strengthens the other’s utility and commercial reach. GOOG’s dominance in the search space provides the real-time ground truth and a vast amount of data, while the Gemini AI tool provides the reasoning and synthesis. In terms of financials, I anticipate existing segments such as search keep generating robust cash flow to fund the continued investments in new segments.

Speaking of the latest AI traffic data, the growth of Anthropic’s (ANTHRO) Claude model was particularly notable (see the following report for more details) and was equally impressive to the Gemini’s growth, as quoted above.

Claude reached 11 million global daily visits, a 308% year-over-year increase and a substantial 63% month-over-month jump. This trend continued in the US market: Claude's US daily visits reached 3.3 million, up 353% year-over-year and 67% month-over-month.

The relationship between Google and Claude a nuanced coopetition in my view, a blend of deep financial partnership and aggressive product rivalry. Google has a sizable stake in Anthropic, and this stake acts as a hedge for GOOG shareholders. If Anthropic’s AI model wins the market, Google shareholder can profit through the stake. Anthropic is also major customer of Google Cloud under the current arrangement, so its success could also drive revenue for Google’s cloud and hardware divisions. However, the rise of Claude could also cause GOOG to lose pentanal clients especially high-value clients. My own experience is that Claude is far better in coding and mathematical tasks, while Gemini shines in more general tasks. As such, I see a potential for high-value customers (engineers, analysts, and researchers) to adapt to Claude in a way similar to how these groups favor MatLab over more general coding/analysis software.

To wrap up, this GOOG revisit was mainly triggered by two new developments since my last writing: the latest disclosures of super-investors’ holdings and the latest AI traffic data. Recent volatility in the broader market has caused GOOG share price to fall below these investors’ average holding price by about 5%. My estimates show a favorable ROI potential at their holding P/E, which becomes even more favorable at the current share prices. The return/risk curve is further skewed by the latest market share data for GOOG’s search and AI tools. With these considerations, I reiterate my Buy rating.

data.title: Adobe: Stop The Wishful Thinking And Face The Reality
data.description:
Adobe: One Way Street (Downwards)

The mood among Adobe Inc. (ADBE) investors right now must be highly depressing.

When we consider the long-term chart showing the "one-way street" in its price action, I don't think any of us would disagree.

What worries me more is that the decline happened since Q3 of 2024, suggesting a multi-year digestion of its premium multiple hasn't been adequately addressed.

For now, a near-term bottom appears to be more likely than not. But, should investors contemplate returning right now?

Adobe: Big AI Spenders Are Looking Elsewhere

More intense competitive challenges have already worried Adobe investors. We already know that Figma (FIG) and Canva are proving more than being capable rivals.

Right now, they are clearly also attracting higher AI spending among larger enterprise customers at the expense of Adobe.

From the above table, we can see why, as ADBE notched a -15% YoY change metric.

Of course, there's no need to freak out all of a sudden right now. But, these are justified concerns and something that we cannot simply dismiss (no longer).

Still, it's helpful to note that Adobe also posted more than $26 billion in ARR recently. So, while the forthcoming threats are real, they don't pose an imminent disruption, yet.

Moreover, while the active user base for Adobe’s smaller peers is growing quickly, Canva ($4B annualized revenue as of the end of 2025) and Figma (>$1 billion for FY2025), this revenue is nowhere close to threatening it yet, right?

Hence, even as the market assumes higher implied growth multiples for Canva and Figma, we also have to recognize that all of them face similar disruption risks from pure play AI LLMs.

But for us to suggest that Adobe is surely doomed is too big a call to make, because I haven’t seen that thesis playing out in its core P&L performances just yet.

Still, I suggested in my previous ADBE writeup, the problems could be worse than we had thought.

And due to these simmering uncertainties, the market could place it under the microscope for an extended duration to see how this AI disruption thesis plays out.

Furthermore, the lackluster performance as observed in the large AI spenders table may continue to put persistent downward pressure on the stock.

That's a handful for investors to handle because Adobe’s business model is obviously coming under pressure from its most direct (but smaller) rivals.

And now, we have to manage transition issues with management, as we know that CEO Shantanu Narayen has already decided to step down once they have managed to identify his successor.

I don't think there's a need for us to speculate further on why Narayen decided to hand over his long-held role in the firm, as the ongoing SaaS disruptive themes might benefit from new leadership perspectives.

ADBE: Value Stock Or Value Trap?

The market’s “judgment” on Adobe’s AI transformation plans under Narayen's leadership so far has been a resounding “poor,” as its implied growth premium has been wiped out.

Trading at just over 10x forward earnings, one might now think about ADBE as a deep value play or a value trap.

Compared to the SaaS median multiple (IGV) of just under 19x, the disconnect is glaring, and the loss of confidence astounding, if you know what I mean.

And when we consider that ADBE’s valuation has dropped to around the $100 billion mark, I’m not sure whether I could even term it as a software behemoth these days.

Is the market expecting the company’s position within the creative software suite to finally be untangled in the Generative AI era?

Adobe's P&L Still Look Pretty Solid

Which side you are standing on will really depend on whether you think Adobe’s much prized margins over the software industry still hold true.

Or could the company now be compelled to lower its high margins to compete even more aggressively against peers and AI companies that are going after its businesses?

Can Adobe lower its margins without suffering another investor exodus?

On a GAAP basis, it posted almost 90% gross margins. Operating margins were also 37% last year. And when I compare that to the 5-year average of 36%, I hardly see stress sensors blaring out loud, right?

It’s as if the market has taken on a totally different personality from the person preparing the financial statements for Adobe.

In other words, Adobe’s fundamental results clearly haven’t been threatened ... yet.

That’s right. "Yet" is the big word and assumption here. But we have already seen some notable shifts in large AI spenders, as I enunciated earlier.

Adobe's AI Future Is Now Up In The Air

And AI budgets are expected to become even more pronounced and expanding moving forward, with a clearly growing allocation to AI spend as part of overall tech outlay.

I think there are enough surveys out there reminding us that the disruptive wave isn’t merely theoretical.

And for Adobe, the questions about whether the current margins are still sustainable will now be front and center from here.

I think Narayen is likely leaving the necessary details for Adobe’s transformation to the next boss, although I’m still keen to find out what the company has in store for us at the upcoming April summit.

While ADBE’s incredibly profitable money printing machine should help mitigate a further decline in its well-battered multiples, it’s too early for us to ascertain a bottom.

Nevertheless, I do note that the company is seeing solid traction in using its freemium model to encourage conversion.

Although questions about whether it will slow ARR monetization will continue to dominate the investing discourse, which I believe is likely inevitable.

Adobe thinks that the current transition follows a similar adoption cadence to that of Acrobat, where paid conversion should naturally follow later.

I think this is where the conundrum really is.

Investors need to judge whether Adobe’s AI offerings are strong or robust enough to spur the conversion, but it’s challenging to put a hard number to the monetization metrics right now.

So, I think cautious investors will probably assess that unless Adobe offers tremendous value in terms of pricing to its customers, it might be hard to convince people to pay up, right?

So this freemium model will continue to face substantive pressure, and I don’t think these questions will be resolved anytime soon as the company experiments with the different value propositions for its pricing model.

If Canva and folks (plus the AI disruptors) are ultimately making significant inroads into Adobe’s business, keeping the margins at such high levels no longer seems to make much sense (to me, at least).

Adobe’s gargantuan ARR of $26 billion augments the enterprise opportunity to convince customers to stay and spend. The platform stickiness is there, but for the non-AI use cases, maybe.

For AI spending, it's really hard to ascertain right now. And that's where the main confusion is at, as the market needs to decide on the appropriate multiples to value ADBE.

Buy Or Sell ADBE Stock?

However, the observation remains that Adobe’s Firefly hasn’t quite performed up to par given the current restrictions that have hampered its adoption.

Third party integration has kind of resolved the interim challenges. Yet, the flip side of it also suggests that it’s hard for investors to consider a premium multiple at this rate.

If the creative software market is going to become more fragmented, it still needs to fend off its faster-growing rivals making gains into its segments.

Such intense competition could be inimical to Adobe's prized margins in the long run

For now, while I acknowledge that much pessimism has been priced in, the ambiguities relating to the management change won't be resolved anytime soon.

And the competitive challenges also don’t look like there’s a quick fix in the near term.

In some ways, Adobe really does seem like it’s mired and stuck between a rock and a hard place.

It still needs to convince investors that it could keep whipping out those highly prized margins, although I’m quite skeptical about it.

Which is why I believe the market probably wants us to think that ADBE’s low forward earnings multiples aren’t totally unjustified.

With its competitive moat under threat, I’m not exactly sure how quickly buyers will return to help turn it around anytime soon.

Staying on the sidelines and monitoring the developments for now isn’t just prudent, but also apt, in my opinion.

Rating: Maintain Hold.

data.title: Fifth Third Bancorp: An Income Play With Covered Calls
data.description:
Introduction

U.S. regional banks such as Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB) are holding onto modest gains so far in 2026, outperforming the broader iShares US Financials ETF (IYF). In my opinion, this makes sense since regional banks' exposure to the U.S. economy acts as a tailwind amid energy price volatility, which is primarily impacting energy-importing economies overseas. In contrast, larger U.S. financials, which have more exposure to countries outside the United States, may record weaker EPS growth in 2026.

Against this backdrop, I think FITB shares offer an interesting income play with covered calls. The investment thesis behind the covered call trade can be summarized as:

  • FITB stock trades at a circa 9% forward P/E premium to regional bank peers, arguably presenting limited upside in the near term.
  • At the same time, analysts expect EPS growth to accelerate to roughly 20% in 2027 as benefits from the merger with Comerica kick in.
  • Meanwhile, selling out-of-the-money covered calls provides current income and downside protection against risks, the principal of which is a potential recession in the United States.

2025 Results Recap

FITB delivered EPS of $3.53/share in 2025, a 12.4% Y/Y increase relative to 2024. The principal drivers were a lower share count (circa 2% contribution), an improved efficiency ratio of 56.9% (-2.3% Y/Y), and a higher net interest margin of 3.11% (+0.21% Y/Y). This helped offset marginally higher credit costs.

The bank's CET1 capital improved to 10.77% (+0.2% Y/Y), while the loan-to-deposit ratio was little changed at 72% (2024: 73%). As such, despite solid loan growth of 4.7% Y/Y, deposit inflows and retained earnings helped FITB achieve a higher net interest margin.

All in all, from an operating perspective, I think there were no major red flags at FITB, with focus now turning to 2026 and the effects of the merger with Comerica, which closed in Q1 2026.

2026 Outlook

2026 results will be impacted by costs associated with the Comerica merger, with FITB targeting conversion of systems and branches for H2 2026. As a result, analysts expect EPS growth of about 15% to $4.07/share in 2026, accelerating by roughly 20% to $4.90/share in 2027.

This comes as the bank recently lowered its non-interest income outlook for 2026, most likely driven by weakness in capital markets. As a reminder, FITB's Wealth & Asset Management segment accounts for 17% of FITB fees.

All in all, while some merger costs were absorbed in 2025, the Comerica deal will still present a near-term headwind before synergies begin to contribute in 2027.

Potential To Sell Covered Calls

As I write this, FITB shares trade around $47.11, reflecting a 2026 P/E multiple of 11.58x. This represents a circa 9% premium to regional bank peers, which trade at roughly 10.66x their forward earnings. As such, FITB's valuation clearly reflects expectations for an acceleration in earnings growth once merger costs of 2026 begin to fade and 2027 synergies start.

Against this backdrop, I would rate FITB as a "Hold," advising readers to go for a covered call selling strategy over the coming months. Taking note of higher 2027 EPS expectations and a 12-month price target of $56.10/share, I think selling out-of-the-money covered calls would represent the best risk-reward.

On the one hand, selling call options will generate some current income, with a strike price between $50 and $55 most suitable depending on individual preferences for option income versus capital gain potential. On the other hand, this option income will also provide some loss absorption capacity as FITB shares are arguably somewhat expensive in the near term.

Looking at FITB option prices, we see the June 2026 $50 calls trading at about $1.80 per contract, reduced to $0.53 per contract for the $55 calls. As such, selling out-of-the-money covered calls could generate incremental income of about ~1%-3% for 2.5 months, respectively.

More conservative readers may want to consider the September 2026 calls, which offer somewhat lower option income per month but larger upfront downside protection, as well as smaller rollover risk (which could materialize if we see a substantial change in the price of FITB stock between June/September).

To conclude, while FITB stock arguably offers limited upside in the near term, it remains an interesting opportunity for investors with a 2027 horizon. Meanwhile, selling covered calls can generate incremental income for holders of FITB stock.

Risks

While the covered call strategy can provide downside protection, it still comes with several risks. Firstly, a severe downturn in the U.S. economy may push up credit costs at FITB, dashing any hopes for EPS growth driven by the merger with Comerica. These risks have recently increased somewhat, with Goldman Sachs (GS) increasing the recession probability to 30% over the next 12 months.

If you do decide to go for the covered call selling strategy, you should note that the position may result in a near-term mark-to-market loss if FITB stock volatility increases. Likewise, investors may miss out on capital gain upside beyond their chosen strike price. As such, if one is more bullish on FITB stock, limiting the upside with covered calls does not make sense.

The third principal risk to consider is that the realization of merger synergies depends on a successful integration of Comerica branches and systems in H2 2026. As such, execution risk about the integration should also be on the radar for investors.

Conclusion

FITB stock has managed to post modest gains so far in 2026, in line with regional bank peers. This comes as larger U.S. financials take a hit due to their higher valuations and exposure to overseas markets suffering from energy price volatility.

Analysts expect FITB's earnings growth to accelerate from 2026 to 2027 as the bank puts merger costs associated with the combination with Comerica behind it. That said, FITB's somewhat elevated valuation relative to peers arguably presents limited upside in the near term.

I think this backdrop creates an opportunity to sell out-of-the-money covered calls, providing some current income and downside protection as FITB works on the Comerica integration.

Key risks in the investment case include a potential recession in the United States, mark-to-market losses and limited upside from selling covered calls, as well as execution risks related to the Comerica systems integration.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Asset Class Scoreboard: March 2026
data.description:
March 2026 brought a broad pullback across most asset classes to close out the first quarter. World stocks fell -8.03% and U.S. real estate dropped -6.37%, while U.S. stocks declined -4.93%. These losses pushed U.S. stocks into negative territory for the year at -4.36%, while world stocks held onto a slim +2.01% year-to-date gain despite the difficult month.

Commodities stood as the clear outlier on the March scoreboard, surging +24.23%. Building on gains of +10.49% in January and +1.88% in February, commodities now lead all asset classes by a wide margin with a year-to-date return of +39.84% through the end of Q1.

Managed futures slipped just -0.53% in March. One of the smaller declines on the scoreboard during a month when most risk assets fell sharply. Their year-to-date return remains at +7.35%, placing them second on the 2026 scoreboard behind Commodities and well ahead of the rest of the field.

Hedge funds and bonds also finished March in the red, declining -2.24% and -1.74%, respectively. Both remain positive for the year, though narrowly, hedge funds at +1.82% and bonds at just +0.05%. Cash continued its steady pace with another +0.31% monthly return, bringing its Q1 total to +0.92%. U.S. real estate, despite March’s decline, finished Q1 with a modest +1.00% year-to-date gain.

As Q1 closes, commodities hold a commanding lead on the 2026 scoreboard, with managed futures in a clear second place. U.S. stocks remain the only asset class in negative territory for the year.

Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Sources: Managed Futures = SocGen CTA Index

Cash = US T-Bill 13-week coupon equivalent annual rate/12, with YTD the sum of each month’s value

Bonds = Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (NYSEARCA:BND)

Hedge Funds = IQ Hedge Multi-Strategy Tracker ETF (NYSEARCA:QAI)

Commodities = iShares S&P GSCI Commodity-Indexed Trust ETF (NYSEARCA:GSG)

Real Estate = iShares U.S. Real Estate ETF (NYSEARCA:IYR)

World Stocks = iShares MSCI ACWI ex-U.S. ETF (NASDAQ:ACWX)

US Stocks = SPDR S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:SPY)

All ETF performance data from Y Charts

Disclaimer

The programs listed here are a sub-set of the full list of programs able to be accessed by subscribing to the database and reflect programs we currently work with and/or are more familiar with.

Benchmark index performance is for the constituents of that index only, and does not represent the entire universe of possible investments within that asset class. And further, that there can be limitations and biases to indices such as survivorship, self reporting, and instant history. Individuals cannot invest in the index itself, and actual rates of return may be significantly different and more volatile than those of the index.

Managed futures accounts can be subject to substantial charges for management and advisory fees. The numbers within this website include all such fees, but it may be necessary for those accounts that are subject to these charges to make substantial trading profits in the future to avoid depletion or exhaustion of their assets.

Investors interested in investing with a managed futures program (excepting those programs which are offered exclusively to qualified eligible persons as that term is defined by CFTC regulation 4.7) will be required to receive and sign off on a disclosure document in compliance with certain CFT rules The disclosure documents contains a complete description of the principal risk factors and each fee to be charged to your account by the CTA, as well as the composite performance of accounts under the CTA's management over at least the most recent five years. Investor interested in investing in any of the programs on this website are urged to carefully read these disclosure documents, including, but not limited to the performance information, before investing in any such programs.

Those investors who are qualified eligible persons as that term is defined by CFTC regulation 4.7 and interested in investing in a program exempt from having to provide a disclosure document and considered by the regulations to be sophisticated enough to understand the risks and be able to interpret the accuracy and completeness of any performance information on their own.
